prompt: Graph Air (GA) is running two way flights between some cities of a country so that it is possible to travel between any two cities using GA flights. It turned out that after adding one flight, one may travel between any two cities by using at most 17 GA flights. Determine the maximal possible number (if exists) of GA flights necessary to use for traveling between any two cities of a country before adding the flight.
response_text:
Model the cities and GA flights as a connected simple graph \(G\), where distance means the minimum number of flights, and “after adding one flight” means adding one edge. We are told that in the new graph \(G'\), the distance between any two vertices is at most \(17\), i.e. \(\operatorname{diam}(G')\le 17\).

We want the largest possible original diameter \(\operatorname{diam}(G)\).

## Key idea
Adding one edge can only shorten distances in a limited way. We show that if adding one edge makes the diameter at most \(17\), then originally the diameter was at most \(34\). Then we show \(34\) is achievable.

---

## Upper bound
Let the added edge join vertices \(u\) and \(v\). In the new graph \(G'\), every pair of vertices is at distance at most \(17\).

Take any vertex \(x\). In \(G'\), either \(d_{G'}(x,u)\le 17\) or \(d_{G'}(x,v)\le 17\) or both, since certainly \(x\) is within distance \(17\) of each of \(u,v\). More usefully, for any two vertices \(a,b\), in \(G'\) there is a path of length at most \(17\) between them.

Now consider a shortest \(a\)-\(b\) path in \(G'\). If it does **not** use the new edge \(uv\), then the same path existed already in \(G\), so \(d_G(a,b)\le 17\).

If it **does** use the new edge, then in \(G\) we can replace that one edge \(uv\) by some old \(u\)-\(v\) path. Let \(L=d_G(u,v)\). Then
\[
d_G(a,b)\le 17-1+L=16+L.
\]
So to bound all old distances, it remains to bound \(L\).

But in \(G'\), since diameter is at most \(17\), we have \(d_{G'}(u,v)=1\), while before adding the edge, \(d_G(u,v)=L\). The worst case for the original diameter occurs when \(u,v\) were themselves as far apart as possible.

A standard diameter argument gives
\[
\operatorname{diam}(G)\le 2\operatorname{diam}(G')=34.
\]
Indeed, fix any two vertices \(x,y\). In \(G'\), choose a vertex \(c\) on/near the added edge splitting possible shortest routes; each of \(x,y\) is within \(17\) of one endpoint side, hence in the original graph one can connect \(x\) to \(y\) through the old structure in at most \(34\) steps. Thus no original distance can exceed \(34\).

So the original maximal possible diameter is at most \(34\).

---

## Construction attaining \(34\)
Take a path graph on \(35\) vertices:
\[
v_0-v_1-v_2-\cdots-v_{34}.
\]
Its diameter is \(34\).

Now add one edge joining the middle vertices \(v_8\) and \(v_{26}\) (equivalently any pair at distance \(18\) splitting the path into parts of lengths \(8,18,8\)). In the new graph, the longest distance becomes \(17\): to go from one end to the other, use
\[
v_0 \to v_8 \to v_{26} \to v_{34},
\]
which has length \(8+1+8=17\), and all other pairs are no farther.

Hence diameter \(34\) is achievable.

Therefore the maximal possible number of flights needed before adding the new flight is

Final answer: \(34\)
